title: Per capita plastic waste vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Per capita plastic waste generation rate is measured in kilograms per person per day. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Per capita plastic waste vs. GDP per capita

Per capita plastic waste generation rate is measured in kilograms per person per day. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between per capita plastic waste and GDP per capita for various countries in 2010, with GDP per capita expressed in international-$ at 2021 prices. The horizontal axis uses a logarithmic scale ranging from $1,000 to $100,000, while the vertical axis measures plastic waste from 0 kg to 0.6 kg per person per day. Countries are color-coded by region, including North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania, and circle sizes correspond to population.

A cluster of low-income nations, such as the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Togo, and Tanzania, occupies the bottom-left corner, showing GDP levels below $2,000 and plastic waste generation under 0.05 kg. As GDP increases toward the $5,000 to $10,000 range, countries like India, Indonesia, and China appear with waste levels generally between 0.05 kg and 0.15 kg. In the middle of the chart, nations such as Brazil, Turkey, and Costa Rica sit between $10,000 and $20,000 GDP, with waste generation spanning roughly 0.1 kg to 0.25 kg.

The upper-right section contains high-income economies. The United States, Germany, and the Netherlands display GDP figures between $40,000 and $50,000, with waste generation hovering around 0.3 kg to 0.5 kg. Several smaller island nations and wealthy territories, including Antigua and Barbuda, Kuwait, and Guyana, appear at the very top of the chart, exceeding 0.5 kg of waste per person per day. Meanwhile, some high-GDP countries like Sweden and Brunei remain lower on the vertical axis, generating less than 0.1 kg of waste despite GDP levels exceeding $40,000.
